title: Set up and use fuse-overlayfs for a one-time run
body:
### Issue Description

After any `podman run` command using the `--storage-driver` and `--storage-opt` options with 'fuse-overlayfs', the podman configuration is permanently changed.

The podman run documentation describes how to configure and use 'fuse-overlayfs' for a [single execution](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html#configuring-storage-options-from-the-command-line), but the configuration becomes permanent instead of for a single run.

My single execution consists of:
```bash
CNT_USER=0  # 0(root) or 1000(ubuntu)
MNT_PROGRAM="$(which fuse-overlayfs)"

podman --log-level=warn \
  --storage-driver overlay \
  --storage-opt "overlay.mount_program=$MNT_PROGRAM" \
  run -it --rm \
  --volume "$(pwd):/workdir" \
  --userns=keep-id:uid=$(id -u),gid=$(id -g) \
  --user $CNT_USER \
  ubuntu:24.04 ls -lnd /workdir
```

After running this command, two properties in `podman info` are changed:
```bash
$ podman info | grep -i  'native overlay' && podman info | grep -i  'shift'
    Native Overlay Diff: "false"
    Supports shifting: "true"
```

When running `podman --log-level=debug run busybox /bin/sh` after a "one-time setup" execution, I can observe the following in the log:
```
DEBU[0000] [graphdriver] trying provided driver "overlay" 
DEBU[0000] overlay: storage already configured with a mount-program 
DEBU[0000] backingFs=extfs, projectQuotaSupported=false, useNativeDiff=false, usingMetacopy=false 
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

<details>
<summary>podmanIssue.sh</summary>

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

check_environment() {
  if [ "$EUID" -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "Use podman 'rootless'. Exiting..."
    exit 1
  fi

  if ! command -v fuse-overlayfs &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Need fuse-overlayfs installed. Exiting..."
    exit 1
  fi
}

podman_system_reset() {
  echo; echo "Read with CAUTION!!!"
  echo "Confirm at your own risk."
  echo; echo
  podman system reset
}

check_podman_info() {
  podman info | grep -i  'native overlay' && podman info | grep -i  'shift'
}

run_fuse_overlayfs() {
  CNT_USER=0  # 0(root) or 1000(ubuntu)
  MNT_PROGRAM="$(which fuse-overlayfs)"
  
  podman --log-level=warn \
    --storage-driver overlay \
    --storage-opt "overlay.mount_program=$MNT_PROGRAM" \
    run -it --rm \
    --volume "$(pwd):/workdir" \
    --userns=keep-id:uid=$(id -u),gid=$(id -g) \
    --user $CNT_USER \
    ubuntu:24.04 ls -lnd /workdir
}

run_no_fuse_debug() {
  podman --log-level=debug run busybox /bin/sh
}

check_environment

ARG=$(echo "$1")

case "$ARG" in
  system_reset)
    podman_system_reset
    ;;
  info)
    check_podman_info
    ;;
  one_time_run)
    run_fuse_overlayfs
    ;;
  nofuse_debug)  
    run_no_fuse_debug
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Invalid arARG: '$1'"
    ;;
esac
```

</details>

> [!CAUTION]
> Step one will delete all your containers, images, networks, volumes and podman configurations.. Use at your own risk.

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Reset podman: `./podmanIssue.sh system_reset`
2. Check overlay config:  `./podmanIssue.sh info`
    Show `Native Overlay Diff: "true"` and `Supports shifting: "false"`
3.  Run one-time fuse-overlayfs setup: `./podmanIssue.sh one_time_run`
4. Check overlay config:  `./podmanIssue.sh info`  
     Show `Native Overlay Diff: "false"` and `Supports shifting: "true"`
5. Run another container: `./podmanIssue.sh other_debug`
6. Check overlay config:: `./podmanIssue.sh info` 
    keeps showing `Native Overlay Diff: "false"` and `Supports shifting: "true"`


### Describe the results you received

A `podman run` option to 'one-time setup' changed the podman configuration permanently.

### Describe the results you expected

It is expected that a `podman run` "one-time setup" run will not persistently change the podman configuration.

### podman info output

```yaml

  kernel: 6.12.35
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Same issue with podman 4.9.3 on ubuntu 24.04.

### Additional information

None

comments.body:
fuse-overlayfs uses a different format for storing overlay metadata and handling whiteouts compared to the native overlayfs implementation in the kernel.

These differences can lead to subtle issues if you use the kernel native file system on a directory created with fuse-overlayfs.

That is why we don't support switching back and forth between the two configurations, if you use fuse-overlays then you are stuck with it (until you run a `podman system reset`)

> Step one will delete all your containers, images, networks, volumes and podman configurations.. Use at your own risk.
> 
> Steps to reproduce the issue
> 
> 1. Reset podman: `./podmanIssue.sh system_reset`
> 2. Check overlay config:  `./podmanIssue.sh info`
>    Show `Native Overlay Diff: "true"` and `Supports shifting: "false"`
> 3. Run one-time fuse-overlayfs setup: `./podmanIssue.sh one_time_run`
> 4. Check overlay config:  `./podmanIssue.sh info`
>    Show `Native Overlay Diff: "false"` and `Supports shifting: "true"`
> 5. Run another container: `./podmanIssue.sh other_debug`
> 6. Check overlay config:: `./podmanIssue.sh info`
>    keeps showing `Native Overlay Diff: "false"` and `Supports shifting: "true"`
> 

> fuse-overlayfs uses a different format for storing overlay metadata and handling whiteouts compared to the native overlayfs implementation in the kernel.
> 
> These differences can lead to subtle issues if you use the kernel native file system on a directory created with fuse-overlayfs.
> 
> That is why we don't support switching back and forth between the two configurations, if you use fuse-overlays then you are stuck with it (until you run a `podman system reset`)

@giuseppe, Thank you for your prompt reply.

An additional question.
After performing steps 1 through 6, I uninstalled `fuse-overlayfs` from the host.
Then, I ran a completely new container (which required downloading the image) and it was successful.
`podman run -it --rm --volume "$(pwd):/workdir" --userns=keep-id:uid=$(id -u),gid=$(id -g) --user 0 alpine /bin/sh`

Is it expected that after performing steps 1 through 6, followed by uninstalling `fuse-overlayfs` from the system, Podman should continue to function normally?

What storage driver is Podman using at this point?
Is it the same storage driver that was used in step 5 above (Run another container)?
have you used `podman system reset -f` after removing fuse-overlayfs?

> have you used `podman system reset -f` after removing fuse-overlayfs?

I didn't use `reset`. I just uninstalled `fuse-overlayfs` and ran the new Alpine container.

> that is not enough

I don't think I fully understand how "set up and use fuse-overlayfs for a one-time run" mentioned in [that document ](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html#configuring-storage-options-from-the-command-line) works.

I'd really appreciate it if you could help clarify this. 
Like each storage driver is Podman using at each step above.

that is indeed a wrong example, fuse-overlayfs can't be used as a one-time configuration.

Opened a PR to fix it: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26599

This section in [this document](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman.1.html#rootless-mode) also diverges from my tests.
> In rootless mode, Podman automatically uses the fuse-overlayfs program as the mount_program if installed, as long as the $HOME/.config/containers/storage.conf file was not previously created. 


My rootless Podman, without a pre-existing `$HOME/.config/containers/storage.conf` and `fuse-overlayfs` installed, is not using `fuse-overlayfs` by default (tested on NixOS 25.05 and Ubuntu 24.04).

If I may make a suggestion for the documentation, I think an example guideline for "set up and use fuse-overlayfs for a one-time run" is a very effective concept and might be desired in some situations.

It's possible to achieve this using other global options, as shown in the example below. Note that using `--root` in conjunction with some configurations indeed allows for creating a "one-time setup" guideline.

This is a suggested text to add to the `podman run` documentation, at the end of [this](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html#configuring-storage-options-from-the-command-line) section of the "Examples":

-----

Below is shown how to set up and use fuse-overlayfs for a one-time run of busybox using global options. In rootless containers, `fuse-overlayfs` is especially useful as it provides a userspace overlay storage driver better for rootless idmaps (like `--userns=keep-id`). Requires `fuse-overlayfs` installed on the host:
```
MNT_PROGRAM="$(which fuse-overlayfs)"
TEMP_ROOT="/tmp/podman-storage-$$"

podman --root "$TEMP_ROOT" \
  --storage-driver overlay \
  --storage-opt "overlay.mount_program=$MNT_PROGRAM" \
  run -it --rm \
  --volume "$(pwd):/workdir" \
  --userns=keep-id:uid=$(id -u),gid=$(id -g) \
  --user 0 \
  busybox /bin/sh
```

Be careful, each execution performs a new image download, which can generate overhead; it might be preferable to use `TEMP_ROOT="/tmp/podman-storage"`. Interacting with the environment always requires the `--root` option (e.g., `podman --root "$TEMP_ROOT" ps`, `podman --root "$TEMP_ROOT" image list`). To remove the root: `podman --root "$TEMP_ROOT" system reset` + `rm -rf "$TEMP_ROOT"`.
